problem.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problem.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth.
                
Definition: A binary tree's maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
assumptions: Depth = number of nodes along the longest root-to-leaf path.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Recursive DFS
solutions.description: Recursively compute the depth of the left and right subtrees and return the larger value plus one. The base case is a null node which contributes depth 0. This elegantly expresses the fact that a tree's depth equals the maximum depth of its subtrees plus the root itself.
solutions.code_python:
# class TreeNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.left=self.right=None
from typing import Optional

def max_depth(root: Optional['TreeNode']) -> int:
    if root is None:
        return 0
    return max(max_depth(root.left), max_depth(root.right)) + 1

complexity.space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Iterative BFS
solutions.description: Use level-order traversal to count the number of levels.

complexity.space: O(w) where w is the maximum width

solutions.title: Solution 3 – Iterative DFS with Stack
solutions.description: Use a stack to simulate recursive calls iteratively.
